posts.name: Jim Cowan
posts.cooked:
Recently, I attended a tasting of 30 different Ridge wines of vintages ranging from the 1970’s thru the 2010’s. There were even a couple of whites in the mix.

For many years, my admiration for this house was unbridled but in my crotchety years I have given up buying the wines. The last bottles I bought, I gave away to those for whom the fire still burns.

While almost all of the sites they use are excellent, the farming impeccable and the choice to pick precise, I do not understand the barrel regimen. New American oak seems to be ubiquitous and it’s influence, significant.
On release, many of the reds have such exuberant fruit that one can ignore the ever present sawn-lumber notes. But as they age, the oak tends to obscure the fruit and become paramount. And in my experience, these wines seldom “soak up the oak” such that it’s presence is not obvious. Rather, I think many of the reds become “misshapen” as a result of their barrel treatment.

This tasting reinforced that notion.
Older Zinfandels that I could not recognize as Zin. Carignane with atypical tannin. Even Cabernets that tasted more of generic red wine than of Cab.

Of course, there are exceptions. A 2019 Falanghina was typical of the variety and good (no oak discerned), a couple Monte Bello, Cabernets were good despite their wood notes and the 1997 Monte Bello, Cabernet was a masterpiece.

Aging gives wine a chance to develop. The barrel program at Ridge seems counter-productive.
Aging in a taster often leads to a change in preferences. And so it has.

Best, jim

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked:
Not a comment on OP’s post or Ridge (as I can agree they come across as a little heavy handed at times), but an observation:

There is rampant American Oak skepticism in the US. It’s like some sort of self-flagellation or inferiority complex. A narrative that smells of a bygone era where CA is best “sticking to producing Bordeaux imitation wines”. But if you do it subtly, American Oak is just as elegant as anything else. Yeah, it’s more powerful and its easy to overdo - so don’t.

New generation US wine producers go though contortions to farm organically, dry farm, cover crop, bury cow horns, promote old vineyards, old vines, no fining, no filtering etc only to in the end have no problem with shipping a French Oak barrel across two seas for 5000 miles. Am I the only one who finds that slightly ironic? We should predominantly be using AO here, not FO, if we really cared about our footprint and what we leave behind.

I’m not at all ashamed to say I use only AO on my wines (with the exception of one). AO done right can be magical and undetectable. And if you stylistically absolutely can’t stand any part of AO, from what I’ve heard, Oregon Oak (a different species) behaves more like European oak, so try some of that!

And while I’m not the biggest fan of those lush, sweet vanilla notes typical of American oak (I’m looking at you, Rioja), for some reason La Rioja Alta seems to make wines that are very Rioja with obvious vanilla oak notes, yet I love them. I have no idea how they do it, but they manage to pull off the trick in which most other Rioja wineries fail miserably. It’s some kind of super fine-tuned balance between savory fruit, high acidity, nice tannic backbone and just the right amount of American oak vanilla that intermingles with the fruit without becoming overwhelming or feeling disjointed.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
This got me curious. What is “atypical tannin” in Carignan?

Gritty, grippy and drying.

Ehhh… what?

Carignan is known specifically for its high acidity and very ample, grippy tannins. It was planted throughout Languedoc-Roussillon in the first half of the 20th Century after the phylloxera crisis because it was easy and reliable grower, but it took long time to gain recognition exactly because it used to make very tough, rustic and tannic wines. Only after the local vignerons began to understand more about winemaking and tannin management, the wines started to become more accepted outside the region.

I love Carignan in the local GSMC blends because Carignan normally brings great firmness, acidity and tannins to the wines that can be often a bit soft and flabby without the Carignan component. Varietal Carignan wines tend to be quite tough and tannic.

So I don’t know if you have different clones of Carignan going around, but drying, grippy tannins sounds pretty much exactly what I’d expect from a Carignan. In my books atypical tannins for a Carignan would be those that are soft and mellow. And the entry for Mazuelo (Carignan) in Robinson et al. “Wine Grapes” says:

Black variety from north-east Spain high in tannins and acidity that can make fine wine from old vines but has in its time, as Carignan, blighted Languedoc-Roussillon.

The chapter later says: Mazuelo / Carignan is an extreme variety in every way: high yielding and high in acid, colour and generally tough tannins, occasionally bitter. Even the best examples, the product of old bushvines or softened by carbonic maceration and blended with finer varieties, can provoke mixed responses in wine drinkers.

Ok, as many of you know, I pour wine at Ridge part-time. That being said, I definitely like some wines more than others. No, I’m not gonna say which ones…

It’s real easy to check the oak regimen on their website: Wines - Ridge Vineyards Click on the specific wine and the Winemaking arrow.

Claude got the 2019 Estate Chard wrong. It’s 37% one year old oak.

There are complaints above about recent Geyserville. 2019 only has 20% new oak. 78% two to five year old barrels (remaining 2% is one year old). 2017 and 18, even less new. I checked other vintages back to 2007 (there’s a gap prior to that year). The most I found in any was 24% new. Some in the teens, a couple 11 and 12% new. For me, Geyserville doesn’t get interesting until 10+ years of bottle age. 15-25 is better.

A definite change in the Estate Cab from 2013 forward. 2009-12 in the 20s% new. 2013 and more recent, 35-48% with 2014 at 70%(!) and 2018, 58%.

I love traditional Rioja, but I tend to drink ones that are old. The American oak in those doesn’t phase me in the least.

posts.name: Marshall Manning
posts.cooked:
Ok, as many of you know, I pour wine at Ridge part-time. That being said, I definitely like some wines more than others. No, I’m not gonna say which ones…

It’s real easy to check the oak regimen on their website: Wines - Ridge Vineyards Click on the specific wine and the Winemaking arrow.

Claude got the 2019 Estate Chard wrong. It’s 37% one year old oak.

There are complaints above about recent Geyserville. 2019 only has 20% new oak. 78% two to five year old barrels (remaining 2% is one year old). 2017 and 18, even less new. I checked other vintages back to 2007 (there’s a gap prior to that year). The most I found in any was 24% new. Some in the teens, a couple 11 and 12% new. For me, Geyserville doesn’t get interesting until 10+ years of bottle age. 15-25 is better.

A definite change in the Estate Cab from 2013 forward. 2009-12 in the 20s% new. 2013 and more recent, 35-48% with 2014 at 70%(!) and 2018, 58%.

I love traditional Rioja, but I tend to drink ones that are old. The American oak in those doesn’t phase me in the least.

Too bad they don’t have data for the older vintages on the site, as I would be interested in the wines from the '80s and early '90s. And it’s not just new oak, but a high percentage of 2-3 year old barrels can still make a wine taste oaky, especially for those of us who dislike American oak.

posts.cooked: It seems to me that as the price and popularity of Ridge wines has increased, the amount of people saying they don’t like them has also increased. Purely anecdotal…

Well, it’s time for the WB Ridge slut to weigh in.
I haven’t followed them from the very start… but probably closer than anyone around.
There has always been a significant oak signature in the Ridge wines, talking here about mostly Zin & Cab.
Back in the '70’s, it was very strong and very distinctive, often overwhelming the vnyd source. That is the source of the “Draper perfume” that Geo coined. But it was a distinctive Ridge signature.
In the late '70’s, Ridge developed a severe Brett problem in their cooperage… back when little was understood about brett. Paul thoroughly studied the problem, they threw out most of their cooperage, and the issue was fully resolved by the '84 vintage.
Those reds onward still had a strong Am.oak signature and sort of a “plankiness” that Paul didn’t care for, so they changed their barrel regimen. The Am.oak barrels were crafted to the highest of French standards… split, not sawn staves … air dried rather than kiln dried. The oak barrels are as finely crafted as any European cooperage other than Stockinger.
My sense is, over the last 10-15 yrs is that the intensity of the Am.oak signature has been reduced and the vnyd source signature has been increased. But the Am.oak presence is definitely still there. If you hate Am.oak, these wines are not your bag.
And the Am.oak signature in MonteBello is different from in the Zins/Rhones. It has sort of a coffee bean character. Paul recounts how oftentimes at tastings, people would compliment him on the Fr.oak in the MB and he’d have to explain the oak treatment to them.
But the Am.oak in the Ridge reds is definitely there, to varying levels of intensity. Live with it folks… that’s just Ridge speaking.
Tom

Otto,

To me, this screams of your ‘expectations’ of a variety grown elsewhere not being met with domestic versions. Carignane CAN be a relatively high tannin variety depending upon where it’s grown, when it’s picked, how heavy the crop load is, etc. Traditionally, it has NOT been a heavy tannin wine since it tends to throw a heavier than usual crop and is cherished more for color and fruit then ‘structure’. Your comment about ‘heavy handed winemaking’ is an interesting one, as well as your comment about ‘wines made in the cellar’. Care to give examples of that, please?

Have you had the Sandlands Carignane? My guess is that Tegan is NOT making this in the cellar whatsoever. And if you had a chance to try mine, you’d find the same to be true . . .

Cheers.

It certainly is all about my expectations, since I love Carignan and I’ve had countless different Carignan wines from all around the world, but only a small handful of US Carignanes since they really never make it outside the US. I would be equally surprised if I tasted, say, a Riesling from a place I’d never had wines from before, and that Riesling turned out to be soft and flabby with very low acidity. That would run counter to all I know about Riesling - just like a big and concentrated Carignane with very soft and mellow tannins does.

Regarding your theory on heavier than usual crop; virtually all the sources I’ve read say that Carignan is always known for its rustic, aggressive and even bitter tannins but the variety has been prized exactly because it is reliably produces a very large crop. This has also been historically its biggest problem: due to the high-yielding nature of the variety, the traditional, rustic Carignan wines have been lacking in fruit, concentration and volume - not in acidity or tannins. None of the sources have ever mentioned that heavy crop would soften the tannins, but vice versa: the wines have been unbalanced due to the lack of fruit and concentration to balance out the notable tannins and acidity. I have no idea how Carignane performs in the US, but I’m quite positive most of the Spanish and French producers I’ve talked with would say that your method to have a heavy crop to manage the variety’s inherently ample tannins would be a shortcut to disaster. I wonder if Carignane you grow there is a distinct, less-tannic clone of the Carignan grown everywhere else? I mean, the US Carignanes are the only Carignan wines I’ve tasted that consistently show very soft and mellow tannins.

On the wines showing heavy-handed winemaking, I’d say Cline’s Ancient Vines Carignane was a good example: I don’t know how they vinify the wines, but at 15,5% alcohol, super-ripe fruit and +30% new oak the wine really wasn’t particularly typical of the variety. I served the wine blind to some pretty knowledgeable people and nobody guessed anything remotely close to Carignan (probably due to the very soft and friendly tannins), just commented on how porty and jammy the wine was. The closest call was a modernist Priorat, but even then they added that “probably close to 100% Grenache”. I must admit that the wine was good for such a big and ripe wine and it carried its new oak remarkably well, but there was an agreement that at least this example didn’t feel particularly true to the variety.

And no, I haven’t had your Carignane, nor Sandlands or Tegan. I’d love to, but I guess sourcing those bottles is close to impossible outside the US, unfortunately.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Not a comment on OP’s post or Ridge (as I can agree they come across as a little heavy handed at times), but an observation:

There is rampant American Oak skepticism in the US. It’s like some sort of self-flagellation or inferiority complex. A narrative that smells of a bygone era where CA is best “sticking to producing Bordeaux imitation wines”. But if you do it subtly, American Oak is just as elegant as anything else. Yeah, it’s more powerful and its easy to overdo - so don’t.

New generation US wine producers go though contortions to farm organically, dry farm, cover crop, bury cow horns, promote old vineyards, old vines, no fining, no filtering etc only to in the end have no problem with shipping a French Oak barrel across two seas for 5000 miles. Am I the only one who finds that slightly ironic? We should predominantly be using AO here, not FO, if we really cared about our footprint and what we leave behind.

I’m not at all ashamed to say I use only AO on my wines (with the exception of one). AO done right can be magical and undetectable. And if you stylistically absolutely can’t stand any part of AO, from what I’ve heard, Oregon Oak (a different species) behaves more like European oak, so try some of that!

How about a really novel idea - recycling. Don’t use new oak.

When the wine was young, you could taste the difference between the Charmes and his other wines. They tasted like they were made by two different winemakers. Another faulty assumption, already covered at length in a recent thread. Just how do you know a wine labeled 13.5%, for example, is actually that and not 14.5%? Or more. Some red Burgs reach into 16+ territory and yet labeled way less, way beyond what law allows.

Any data to back this claim?

Having worked for more than half a decade in the local monopoly, where all the wines that come to the selection go through lab checks to confirm that the wines correspond to the analyses that are given in the tenders. I haven’t heard of any Burgundies that would have been past the allowed 0,5% ABV tolerance. The ramifications for a producer in France would be quite big if they get caught in systematically mislabeling their products, so I highly doubt they would label their wines lower in ABV than they actually are just to some bottles to the AWFE crowd. The producers understand that the risk is not worth the effort.

But if you have some actual hard numbers or names to give, I’m all ears!

There’s more play than that in U.S. federal law, so the wines can legally be within 1.5% of the stated number if under 14% ABV and 1% if over. Crossing the threshold into the cheaper category and getting caught is something producers would not intentionally risk. Being on the cusp can be difficult, with label approval being done before final blend, variability between testing methods and so forth. I’ve heard of producers just using a number at the low end of the higher tax category when they are on the cusp and unsure where the wine will end up, but I’ve also heard you can get fined for doing that (paying more tax than required).

Quite a few years ago Adam Lee did an experiment (which got blown way out of proportion and grossly misrepresented in social media) where he blinded Raj Parr on two of his wines. I’ll have to make up the numbers here, but the 15.5% ABV wine seemed lower alcohol and ripeness, no heat. The 13% ABV seemed clearly the riper and more alcoholic of the two.

The topic of ABV and Ridge comes up over and over. People go into hysterics over a single data point. Ridge don’t give a rats ass about brix in their picking decisions, instead going for optimal flavor development, so the ABV range of any long running wine of theirs is about 2.5%. The running average increase in ABV from 1962 to current is .5%. I remember all the Chicken Little squawking threads over the 2001 Monte Bello marking some drastic change. 19 years later, it’s still the outlier.

Similarly, so many cite the '14 Estate Cab as the indicator of a drastic change in oak. It sure was a freakshow of a wine on release (no idea if it has or will improve/integrate with age). Subsequent vintages resemble prior vintages. It’s clear why those barrels were not included in the Monte Bello (which is excellent, as usual, that year).

I used to sell five or six Taransaud barrels every year to them. On my part, it was mostly an excuse to go see Paul and to taste the results. I knew they would never jump up to 50 or 100 per year. About the Raj/Adam story: having been on panels where somebody tried to fool me, let me say that this sort of thing can be annoying:
1/I am nervous enough speaking to a crowd…do I need a trickster out there??
2/There is an unwritten rule you don’t tell the crowd that you think the guy next to you is an idiot. Or that his wine sucks. So who knows what Raj really thought??


About alcohol levels in wine: I am reminded of the EU joke about wine laws. The Italians propose them, the French write them up and the Germans obey them.
I am sure it has happened, but I never heard of a winery getting in trouble for mislabelling alcohol levels. Most import strips are printed years in advance.

I have really fond memories of Ridge Zins from the 70s and 80s (and back when I drank mostly California wine), so I kind of hate to pile on, but this was my impression when my older and now more euro-centric palate cracked a young Monte Bello a few years ago:

  • 2006 Ridge Monte Bello - USA, California, San Francisco Bay, Santa Cruz Mountains (11/25/2010)
    Opened for about five hours before drinking. Sight is typical of a young cab. I imagine there’s a wine hiding under all this American oak, but I couldn’t find it. Undrinkable. Someone please tell me the oak integrates with time because I have five more bottles and they were not cheap. (60 pts.)

I’m going to open another bottle from that same 6-pack tomorrow to drink alongside a Hundred Acre that a good friend wants to share. I’ll report back on how the 2006 is doing.

I’m going to open another bottle from that same 6-pack tomorrow to drink alongside a Hundred Acre that a good friend wants to share. I’ll report back on how the 2006 is doing.

Monte Bello tends to drink well for a little while on release, then shut down hard. Based on their typical performance, four years out would be the absolute worst time to open one. It might drink well now, maybe, but should still be incredibly young. Worth checking in now, since you’ll still have 4 more. Depending how it shows, you might want to wait five or ten years until you pop the next.

Posted from CellarTracker

I’m going to open another bottle from that same 6-pack tomorrow to drink alongside a Hundred Acre that a good friend wants to share. I’ll report back on how the 2006 is doing.

Sounds like you have an axe to grind? You tasted the wine once 11 years ago. Meanwhile there are 182 tasting notes posted in CT, your score of 60 is the low, the next lowest is 88 points up to a high of 98 points. Average is low 90’s. And the professional tasters seem to like the wine just fine.

Pros:
By Antonio Galloni
Vinous, Ridge Monte Bello Revisited: 1968 – 2012 (Jul 2016) (7/1/2016)
(Ridge Monte Bello Central Coast) A dark, brooding beauty, the 2006 Monte Bello is still very, very young. The aromatics alone are enticing, but the tannins still need time to soften. The 2006 is a masculine, vertical Monte Bello that demands cellaring. Even so, it is pretty impressive today. Scents of lavender, sage, mint and savory herbs add closing flourishes. The perfumed sweetness of the fruit - one of the hallmarks of the very late harvest - remains one of the signatures of the 2006. The 2006 can be enjoyed today or cellared for another 25+ years. (Drink between 2016-2046) 95+ points

By Josh Raynolds
Vinous, November/December 2009, IWC Issue #147
(Ridge Vineyards Monte Bello Santa Cruz Mountains) ($145; 68% cabernet sauvignon, 20% merlot, 10% petit verdot and 2% cabernet franc) Deep, bright ruby. Spicy, high-pitched aromas of red- and blackcurrant, cherry, pipe tobacco, rose and smoky Indian spices. Firm, mineral-driven dark berry flavors are given a firm edge by bitter cherry skin and pit notes, with the tobacco note repeating. Supple tannins add structure and carry through the long, sweet, smoky finish. This is showing gentler tannins than I usually expect from a young Monte Bello and I’d bet on it being drinkable before the 2005 and 2004 versions. I also retasted the 2005 Monte Bello, which after a year in bottle is showing brighter red fruit and spicier mineral character than at this time last year, when I rated it 95 points. It’s still a baby. I underestimated this wine’s potential and would now rate it 95(+?), with excellent cellaring potential. For the quality it is a steal as it can compare to any wine from the Napa Valley that sells for three or four times the money-or more! 93 points

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The question I have for the wines coming into Canada - how many labels have to be changed or altered based on their strict labeling laws? Every item coming into Canada is tested, and if it falls outside the regulations, the winery is required to relabel or put a sticker over the alcohol level . . .

Cheers!

This is a very pertinent question, because there is actually a paper published based on this data: Splendide Mendax: False Label Claims About High and Rising Alcohol Content of Wine, which appeared in Journal of Wine Economics, Volume 10, Number 3, 2015, Pages 275–313.

It is worth a read, but it is not in the public domaine.

For the purposes of this discussion, however, it is relevant that for 57% of the wines they looked at (52,178 wines out of 91,432), the alcohol level was under-reported by more than 0.01 per cent. For these wines, “the average actual alcohol content was 13.56% and the average reported alcohol content was 13.15% with an average discrepancy of 0.42 percentage points”. The most under-reported category was, unsurprisingly, New World reds.

If 0.42 per cent is the average discrepancy for 52,178 wines, it must be said that it is remarkable that Otto, working at the monopoly, has never come across a wine that didn’t exceed the 0.5 per cent tolerance…

posts.name: James Sanders
posts.cooked:
[/quote]Sounds like you have an axe to grind? You tasted the wine once 11 years ago. Meanwhile there are 182 tasting notes posted in CT, your score of 60 is the low, the next lowest is 88 points up to a high of 98 points. Average is low 90’s. And the professional tasters seem to like the wine just fine.
[/quote]

Nope. Nothing but years of good feelings towards Ridge. I had a 2002 in 2008 from a .375 and this was my note:

5/27/2009 - I WROTE: 93 points (Edit)
From a .375. Wonderfully complex with red fruits (sour cherry) and menthol/camphor notes. Medium bodied and lively. Structure comes more from acid than tannin. A wonderful lift on the back end, vaguely reminiscent of some older Bosconia’s I’ve had lately. Finish was a bit short.

The 2006 was undrinkably oaky. Period. I let it go for three days and it never resolved. I poured half a bottle down the drain. And, no, it wasn’t flawed.

I’m going to give Wes’s theory some credit and I’ll approach tonight’s bottle with an open mind. American oak does resolve. I have had plenty of LdH and Rioja Alta where the oak integrated nicely.

I’m going to presume you are kidding when you suggest I should change my note because others see it differently. That’s not how this works.
